items.q: Can a single cold winter disprove global climate change?
items.correct: No — climate is the long-term average of conditions over about 30 years, while weather varies year to year; one cold season fits within a warming trend.
items.misconception: A record cold spell shows the planet is not warming.
items.random_wrong: Cold winters are caused by the Moon moving closer to Earth.
items.why: Weather-climate confusion underpins many flawed arguments in the climate change debate.

items.q: Is famine usually caused simply by there being no food?
items.correct: Rarely — famines more often result from failures of access and entitlement: poverty, conflict and broken distribution, sometimes while food is still being exported.
items.misconception: Famine happens only when a country physically runs out of food.
items.random_wrong: Famines occur when people choose not to eat.
items.why: The entitlement insight reframes food security from production to access — a key point in the Future of Food debate.

items.q: Do earthquakes and eruptions kill mainly because of their magnitude?
items.correct: Not alone — death tolls depend heavily on vulnerability: building quality, warning systems, governance and wealth, which is why similar magnitudes kill very differently.
items.misconception: The biggest-magnitude events are always the deadliest.
items.random_wrong: Earthquakes only harm people standing outdoors.
items.why: Hazard does not equal disaster; the role of vulnerability is the core of the Hazardous Earth debate.

items.correct: Oceans absorbing carbon dioxide, which forms carbonic acid and lowers pH
items.random_wrong: Warmer water becoming acidic by itself
items.why: Correct answer: Oceans absorbing carbon dioxide, which forms carbonic acid and lowers pH. “Plastic waste dissolving in seawater” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: John Snow's famous 1854 cholera map demonstrated that:
items.correct: The outbreak was linked to a contaminated water pump
items.why: It's The outbreak was linked to a contaminated water pump. “Cholera spread through the air” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Food security is best defined as:
items.correct: Reliable access for all people to sufficient, safe and nutritious food
items.misconception: A country growing all of its own food
items.random_wrong: Keeping large food stockpiles in warehouses
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Reliable access for all people to sufficient, safe and nutritious food. “A country growing all of its own food” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Reducing greenhouse gas emissions to tackle the cause
items.misconception: Building sea walls against rising seas
items.why: Correct answer: Reducing greenhouse gas emissions to tackle the cause. “Building sea walls against rising seas” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Atmospheric CO2 was about 280 ppm before the industrial revolution and has passed about 420 ppm in recent years. What is the increase in ppm?
items.why: Exact answer: 140 ppm. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
